author: @rosencain
text:
That trainer is about to have one heck of an investigation.
You do NOT hang up - ever.
You do not rush a person who may not be able to speak as fast or as clearly.

Back in like 2002 I had an online friend who was a 911 dispatcher who lived on the East coast (USA) and she had to report a co-worker who would hang up on people she felt were not Americans and those she felt were not the "right" color.

Huge investigation, no deaths, no delays that compounded emergencies or medical attention, but it was a scandal in the area. If there had been one death she would have been brought up on charges.
video_title: Being an operator requires strong psychological resilience #fyp

author: @addictiontransfer3731
text:
While there are countless really good police dispatchers, just one bad one can cause a ton of damage.  Anyone who wants to feel primal rage go look up the 911 call of Debra Stevens. She drowned in her car while the police dispatcher laughed at her, called her annoying, blamed her for getting caught in a flash flood, and neglected to convey the seriousness of the situation to responding officers, ultimately leading to them not finding her. 

Another one is Kyle Plush, kid who was trapped upside down amd awkwardly squeezed between the seats in his car and who called 911 to explain his situation while suffocating to death. The police dispatcher failed to tell the cops that he was trapped in his car and just told them "look for anything abnormal". The cops never even got out of their car, the kid died slowly in blistering heat with no air while begging the dispatcher for help.

author: @shaunbickley3939
text: 2                  Sylvette Herry, better known by her stage name Miou-Miou, is a highly acclaimed French actress. Born on February 22, 1950, in Paris, she is a ten-time César Award nominee and won the Best Actress award for her role in the 1979 film Memoirs of a French Whore.Key Career HighlightsFilm Debut: Started in 1971 with La vie sentimentale de Georges le tueur and La Cavale.Breakthrough Role: Gained international recognition for her work in the Spaghetti Western comedy A Genius, Two Friends, and an Idiot (1975).Major Accolade: Won the César Award for Best Actress (Meilleure actrice) for La Dérobade (Memoirs of a French Whore) in 1980.Notable Films: Known for classic French cinema such as Buffet froid (1979) and Netchaïev est de retour

Sometimes builders just build on top of the existing features rather than clear them out and replace the existing ones if it's cheaper or safer (no pun intended).

author: @samanthawoods8000
text:
“How did the smoke just stay in one section?”

Southerner here. It didn’t. The non-smoking section was just LESS smokey.

Some restaurants were divided into two rooms, and one room was the smoking section. However, there wasn’t a door or between them (typically), so the smoke would still trickle into the non-smoking section.

In other places, the smoking section was just a space in the restaurant. There were no glass dividers, air filters, or anything like that. So, the non-smoking section just meant you weren’t sitting inside the cloud of smoke.
video_title: Smoking Section

author: @caramelcrunchy9927
text: He escaped the loop because he understood that no matter what he does with his time, he will die. Trying to save another man's life over and over but realizing you could never do anything to stop the truth of mortality helped him see that he needed to spend his time more efficiently. That's when he found his love for the girl, and finally took a chance getting to know her instead of wasting his life alone

author: @ln.temperr5157
text: Growing up this scene played a big part into who I am now. The amount of lunch breaks I've gone without eating having just given what I had to a homeless dude, Sat and had a cigarette while listening to their story, before heading back to work. Its a simple pleasure knowing you made that day a little less shit for them, a little loney. All cause I know how fast it could be me in that situation, and I'd hope someone would do the same for me if so. Be kind people, there is no reward better than a sad persons smile.

author: @GiovanniCheeks-r4j
text:
The F-16 Fighting Falcon is one of the most successful and widely used fighter jets in the world, designed to be fast, agile, and versatile. Developed in the 1970s by General Dynamics for the United States Air Force, the F-16 was created to give pilots a lightweight but powerful aircraft capable of excelling in air-to-air combat and ground attack missions. Its design philosophy focused on efficiency, maneuverability, and affordability compared to heavier fighter jets. Because of this balance, the F-16 has remained relevant for decades and is still used by many countries today.

One of the key reasons the F-16 is so effective is its aerodynamic design. The jet has a sleek fuselage, blended wing body, and a frameless bubble canopy that gives the pilot excellent visibility. Its wings are shaped to allow high maneuverability at both low and high speeds, making it especially strong in dogfights. The aircraft was also designed with a relaxed static stability concept, meaning it is naturally unstable in flight, which allows it to turn more sharply and respond faster to pilot input than older fighter jets.

The materials that make up the F-16 play a major role in its performance. The airframe is built using a combination of aluminum alloys, titanium, steel, and composite materials. Aluminum keeps the jet lightweight, while titanium is used in areas that experience extreme heat and stress, such as near the engine. Composite materials help reduce weight and increase strength, which improves speed, fuel efficiency, and overall durability. These materials together allow the F-16 to withstand intense forces during high-G maneuvers.

What truly makes the F-16 “run” is its powerful jet engine. Most F-16s are equipped with either a Pratt & Whitney or General Electric turbofan engine. This engine produces tens of thousands of pounds of thrust, enabling the jet to exceed the speed of sound. The engine works by drawing in air, compressing it, mixing it with fuel, and igniting it to produce a high-velocity exhaust that pushes the aircraft forward. This thrust gives the F-16 its impressive acceleration and climbing ability.

In addition to its engine, the F-16 relies heavily on advanced avionics to function effectively. Avionics include radar systems, navigation tools, communication equipment, and onboard computers. These systems help pilots detect enemy aircraft, track targets, and deliver weapons with high precision. Over the years, upgrades have added digital displays, improved radar, and modern sensors, allowing the F-16 to operate in complex combat environments and alongside newer aircraft.

Another major factor that makes the F-16 work so well is its fly-by-wire flight control system. Instead of direct mechanical connections, the pilot’s inputs are sent electronically to computers, which then adjust the control surfaces. This system constantly makes tiny corrections to keep the jet stable and responsive. It allows the aircraft to safely fly with its intentionally unstable design and helps pilots perform extreme maneuvers that would be impossible in older jets.

In conclusion, the F-16 fighter jet is a combination of smart design, strong materials, powerful propulsion, and advanced technology. It was built to be lightweight, fast, and adaptable, making it effective in many different combat roles. Its engine provides the raw power, while its avionics and flight control systems ensure precision and control. Together, these elements explain why the F-16 has remained a cornerstone of modern air forces and continues to be respected as one of the most capable fighter jets ever built.

Remedy
2014 ‧ Drama ‧ 2 seasons

Canadian medical drama that aired for two seasons (2014-2015) on Global in Canada and Ovation in the U.S., focusing on the Conner family, who all work at Toronto's Bethune General Hospital. The series offers an "upstairs/downstairs" perspective, following the lives of the three Conner siblings—a surgeon, a nurse, and a med school dropout working as an orderly—and their physician father, the acting Chief-of-Staff. It explores the high-stakes world of medicine through the unique family dynamic, contrasting the "downstairs" staff with the "upstairs" doctors.

author: @Data_10000
text:
My grandma was an RN. She actually studied medicine all the way up at a time when women were disuaded from becoming doctors. She then went on to get her LPN. So she worked both from home and as an instructor at a mental institution where she met my grandfather. They liked to point out that he was a porter, not a patient, because it wasn't obvious. A lot of the doctors dismissed my grandpa but my grandma would call him or the other porters when there was an issue with the patients, not the doctors. She taught nursing students and even intern doctors that no one in the facility knows the patients as well as the porters do because the patients will speak freely with them where as they will remain guarded with any medical staff for a really long time until trust is built. My grandpa and the other porters could calm the patients faster than any doctor without medicinal intervention 9/10 times. Doctors were only called in if the porters failed. That is how she ran things there. She could have become a doctor but chose to get all of the nursing certificates instead (I mean all of them) because it gave her a more humane relationship with the patients. 
By the time I came into this world, my grandma ran a nursing home out of her house. When I was 8 she moved and started doing her LPN work out of her house after registering with a local clinic. She never stepped foot in the clinic itself, instead she did all of the house calls (think call the midwife and the district rounds). She taught me diagnostics and soap notes. I used to go with her and hold her bag or keep her patients comfortable while she took care of them. Now I work in the in-home supportive services field and my vision, who also would go out with her or with us, is a doctor working in cancer research. I chose this field because her words to me when I went with her the first time "your grandpa can't be my porter anymore and that is the most important job. You treat the human aspect, I treat the medical. Both are necessary, however the porter can work without the nurse or doctor, but the doctor cannot do their job without a porter."

author: @PaulHosler
text: That's cool! I had the same idea when I was 10 and a bunch of us kids from the neighborhood got together to make a haunted house. I had the bright idea to throw a spear at people as they walked in to the entrance. I had a rubber tipped spear from som amusement park, I put some metal guides on it, threaded some heavy fishing line through the guides, and strung up the whole thing on a bit of a slope. The operator could give the spear a little push and it would fly across the room and hit a spot next to the entrance. It was a huge hit, and great fun! Oh, this was 1977... sadly I didn't pursue a career as a props guy.

Based on the definition of medicine the charges were dropped. Hence, when Dave took his friend he still died of AIDS. My father believed in his products and died from cancer when it returned. His initial treatment he followed Veterans Administration Hopsital protocol and his cancer was in remission. When it returned he decided to "be cured" by Dr. Sebi. Do you get the joke?

text:
Accepting the premise is the issue, imo. I dont think I am capable of caring how a billionaire feels about their own money. 
You have more than you could POSSIBLY need in a lifetime and your priority is only getting more, that is a sickness, not a lifestyle.

It would be one thing if JB had cured cancer successfully and ended it entirely. Or if he ended world hunger. This man did not provide a benefit to the human race that meets his worth. Convenience isn’t the same as meaningful improvement and he has a lot to make up for to the general public for what his “convenience services” have created for average Americans.

author: @da1trugamr
text:
Y'know, now that I think about it... Is young Sheldon one of the first shows like.... Ever... To be so continuously and consistently filmed that you actually get to WATCH of the actors grow up? 

The more I think on it there's always like a... 2 year gap between seasons or it's a movie series like Harry Potter where sure you do watch them grow up... But there's so many years in between filming that you can tell from movie to movie that he's gotten older. 

Like this is gonna come off as cringey but it's genuinely heartwarming literally seeing Georgie start as a teen and grow into a good young man. I can't remember another show where you get to watch in real time as the characters grow up.
